ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002473 'fish sandwich' is the closest available match; no exact FoodOn term exists for 'crispy fish burger' as a distinct breaded-fish burger preparation.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP 'Fish burger' is the closest defensible FoodEx2 code; the specific garlic bun and coleslaw accompaniment are not captured at this level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to a crispy fish burger dish at a clinically useful level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of a crispy fish burger with coleslaw and fries weighs approximately 350 g, delivering around 920 kcal, 46.6 g protein, 35.6 g fat, and 103.5 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Crispy Fish Burger — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Crispy Fish Burger (cr_id G00010456). All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on white bread bun and breaded fish composite
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a crispy fish burger as a composite dish. GI estimated at 66 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate contributors: white bread bun (GI ~70–75, University of Sydney GI Database) and breaded white fish coating (GI ~55–60 analogous to battered/breaded fish products). Weighted composite estimate yields GI ~66. Glycemic load per 350 g serving calculated as: (66 × 29.57 g carbohydrate per 100g × 3.5) / 100 = 19.1 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium (487 mg/100g) with insufficient potassium (197 mg/100g) and dietary fibre (1.8 g/100g) to offset sodium load
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,705 mg sodium — exceeding 70% of the 2,400 mg daily limit — and the low potassium-to-sodium ratio provides no meaningful cardiovascular offset; request grilled fish, reduce or omit sauces, and replace fries with a salad to substantially lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (29.57 g/100g) from white bun and breadcrumb coating with low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100g), producing a medium-to-high glycaemic impact; estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100g) from deep frying worsens insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this dish in its standard form; the refined white bun and breadcrumb coating create a medium GI (66) with a glycaemic load of ~19 per serving, and the estimated trans fat from deep frying further impairs insulin sensitivity — substituting a wholegrain bun, requesting grilled fish, and omitting fries can meaningfully reduce the glycaemic and metabolic burden.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generating trans fat (~0.2 g/100g) and oxidised fats, combined with high sodium (487 mg/100g) and saturated fat (~2.1 g/100g), elevates LDL cholesterol and arterial inflammation risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish; the combination of trans fat from deep frying, high sodium, and saturated fat in a single serving creates a compounded cardiovascular risk — choosing a grilled fish fillet on a wholegrain bun with a side salad instead of fries would substantially reduce trans fat, saturated fat, and sodium exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and trans fat (~0.2 g/100g), both of which are direct pro-inflammatory triggers for rheumatoid arthritis
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; AGEs from deep frying and estimated trans fat at 0.2 g/100g are established joint-inflammation triggers — replacing with a grilled fish fillet and omitting tartar sauce and pickles would significantly reduce the inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Crispy Fish Burger is a Western-style fast-food dish popular in Singapore, consisting of a deep-fried breaded white fish fillet served in a toasted garlic bun with sliced tomato, and accompanied by homemade coleslaw and french fries. The fish fillet is coated in seasoned breadcrumbs and deep-fried until golden and crunchy, yielding a crisp exterior with a moist, flaky interior. The garlic bun adds a savoury aromatic note, while the coleslaw provides a creamy, slightly tangy contrast. Per 100 g, the dish provides 263 kcal, 13.3 g protein, 10.18 g fat, and 29.57 g carbohydrate, with 487 mg sodium and 1.8 g dietary fibre. The high sodium content (487 mg/100g), low fibre, and estimated trans fat from deep frying make this dish a high-concern food for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The refined white bun and breadcrumb coating produce a medium glycaemic index of approximately 66, with a glycaemic load of ~19 per standard 350 g serving. Clinically, this dish is best consumed occasionally and modified — grilled fish on a wholegrain bun with a salad side — to reduce sodium, trans fat, and glycaemic impact. It is commonly served at lunch and dinner in Western fast-food outlets and casual dining restaurants across Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a fish burger?
Can diabetics eat a fish burger?
Is fish burger high in sodium?
What is the GI of a fish burger?
Is crispy fish burger suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Crispy Fish Burger (cr_id G00010456) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. All core macros and key micronutrients are present from this primary approved source. GI is an analogous estimate documented with University of Sydney GI Database references. Saturated fat and trans fat are estimated from deep-fried breaded fish composition analogues in USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) and are clearly documented.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish is not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database entry; USDA FoodData Central does not carry an exact match for this Singapore-specific preparation. 2. trans_fat_g is set to 0.2 g/100g as an estimate based on deep-fried breaded fish analogues in USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy item 'Fish fillet, battered or breaded, fried'); this is not directly reported by HPB and is flagged as an estimate. 3. saturated_fat_g of 2.1 g/100g is estimated from USDA SR Legacy analogous deep-fried breaded fish fillet data; not directly reported by HPB. 4. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish and no exact-match approved source provides them for this specific Singapore preparation. 5. GI of 66 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for a crispy fish burger exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Halal status is assumed based on typical Singapore Western fast-food fish burger preparations using white fish; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 7. All core macros (protein_g 13.3, fat_g 10.18, carbohydrate_g 29.57) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification is required.
